summary: This comprehensive essay traces the evolution of software development methodologies from chaotic 'code and fix' approaches through plan-driven 'monumental' methodologies to agile methods. Fowler argues that agile methodologies are fundamentally different from traditional approaches in two key ways: they are adaptive rather than predictive (embracing change rather than resisting it), and people-oriented rather than process-oriented (recognizing that talented developers cannot be treated as interchangeable resources). The essay explains why software development cannot follow the engineering metaphor of separating design from construction, since in software, construction (compilation) is essentially free and all effort is design work. Fowler surveys various agile approaches including XP, Scrum, Crystal, and Lean Development, while emphasizing that the core principles unite them despite surface differences.
key_points: Software development fundamentally differs from civil engineering because construction is essentially free (compilation) while design constitutes nearly all the work, making the traditional engineering metaphor inappropriate

Agile methods embrace two core principles: adaptivity (welcoming change rather than resisting it through detailed upfront planning) and people-orientation (treating developers as skilled professionals rather than replaceable resources)
Requirements instability is the norm in business software, not an aberration to be prevented through better requirements engineering
Iterative development with frequent working software releases provides the honest feedback mechanism needed to control unpredictable processes
Measurement-based management leads to dysfunction in creative knowledge work; delegatory management where practitioners decide how to do their work is more effective for software development
money_quotes: Creative processes are not easily planned, and so predictability may well be an impossible target.
In software: construction is so cheap as to be free. In software all the effort is design, and thus requires creative and talented people.
A late change in requirements is a competitive advantage.
The people involved are not replaceable parts.

If you expect all your developers to be plug-compatible programming units, you don't try to treat them as individuals. This lowers morale (and productivity). The good people look for a better place to be, and you end up with what you desire: plug-compatible programming units.
There is nothing more frustrating to a developer than seeing their hard work go to waste.
One of the big dangers is to pretend that you can follow a predictable process when you can't.
Documents can hide all sorts of flaws. Untested code can hide plenty of flaws. But when people actually sit in front of a system and work with it, then flaws become truly apparent.
key_insight: Software development is fundamentally a design activity where construction costs are negligible, which means it requires adaptive, people-centered processes rather than the predictive, plan-driven approaches borrowed from traditional engineering disciplines.
